reasoning:
Country level: The scene shows multiple strong German cues. The emergency doctor vehicle has the word “NOTARZT” and the EU emergency number “112,” a livery style widely used in Germany. The bus stop uses the distinctive German “H” (Haltestelle) symbol on a white oval sign with green/yellow ring. Vehicles have EU-style plates consistent with German formatting, and the built environment (paving stones, curb design, traffic signal heads) matches Germany. Together these exclude neighboring countries with different bus stop symbols (e.g., Austria’s different logo, Netherlands’ blue signs). 
City level: The presence of in-street rail tracks indicates a tram network. Many German cities have trams, but the overall look—postwar mid-rise housing blocks, mature street trees, and Ruhr-style street furniture—points toward North Rhine–Westphalia. The emergency vehicle color scheme (white/orange with “NOTARZT”) and the bus stop flag layout resemble those commonly seen in the Ruhr area (Essen/Duisburg/Gelsenkirchen). There is no unique landmark to assert a specific city, but Ruhr characteristics make NRW more plausible than southern or eastern Germany. 
Street level: The carriageway includes embedded tram tracks with a side platform bus/tram stop directly beside a low blue-and-glass car-wash building bearing large “Super Wash” letters. A grey signal pole stands at the crossing with short zebra markings across a minor side street. Low hedges, a graffiti-tagged retaining wall, and a mid-century apartment block frame the stop. These features describe a specific curbside stop next to a car wash along a tram corridor, narrowing within the chosen city though still not uniquely identifiable without readable stop names.

presence of on-street tramway indicating cities with tram networks (common in Germany, Ruhr area)
